summary: Big news from the SE BeltLine corridor as Reynoldstown finally sees development break ground near the trail and Inman Park welcomes a new Asian-American comfort food spot. Meanwhile, Atlanta's dealing with airport chaos as ICE agents patrol Hartsfield-Jackson amid government shutdown woes.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown Corner Near Beltline Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After nearly five years of sitting as cleared lots, a mixed-use project at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace is finally moving forward. Atlanta-based Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers to build 85 residential units just a block from the Eastside Trail. It's exactly the kind of development Reynoldstown neighbors have been waiting to see.
topStory.body:
Remember when those lots at Chester and Fulton got cleared way back in 2021? Yeah, we all wondered what was taking so long. Well, the good news is that Embry Development Company has finally started construction on what they're calling Camber Crossing, bringing 85 townhomes and condos to that prime corner just a block west of the Beltline.

This is the kind of project that makes perfect sense for Reynoldstown. You've got easy access to the trail, Memorial Drive's getting better, and the neighborhood's been hungry for quality housing options that aren't completely out of reach. Embry took over the permitted project from national developer Toll Brothers two years ago, and while it took them a while to get going, they seem committed to actually finishing what they started.

What's really encouraging is seeing local developers step in where the big national guys didn't follow through. Reynoldstown has been one of those neighborhoods that's been on the verge of its moment for years now, and projects like this could be the tipping point that brings more investment and energy to the area.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site at 195 Chester Avenue showing groundwork beginning for Camber Crossing development in Reynoldstown

stories.imageAlt: Construction site at 195 Chester Avenue showing groundwork beginning for Camber Crossing development in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur's 'Missing Middle' Housing Village Gets Ready to Launch
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is about to soft-launch next week, bringing 18 for-sale houses built around shared courtyards near downtown Decatur. With median home values hitting over $700,000 in the area, this village-style development aims to offer more accessible single-family options ITP. It's the kind of creative infill housing we need more of.
stories.body:
You know how everyone's always talking about the 'missing middle' in housing? Well, here's a project that's actually doing something about it. Cottages at Midway on Midway Road is turning two underused lots into 18 standalone houses arranged around shared green space, and they're targeting buyers who've been priced out of the regular Decatur market.

This isn't your typical suburban subdivision. The houses are designed to feel like a real neighborhood, with front porches facing common areas and the kind of walkable scale that makes sense near a major commercial corridor. When you're competing with $700,000+ median home prices in Decatur proper, creating something more attainable without going OTP is exactly what we need.

The soft launch starting next week should give us a better sense of pricing and whether this model can actually work. If it does, hopefully we'll see more developers taking the village approach to infill housing instead of just cramming in as many units as possible.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan showing layout of 18 houses at Cottages at Midway development in Decatur

stories.headline: West End's Rare Beltline Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is bringing 23 for-sale townhomes to the West End along the Beltline, with the first seven units nearing completion. Developed by the same folks behind Lee + White, these are some of the few new for-sale options along this stretch of the trail. The first units should hit the market this spring.
stories.body:
If you've been walking the Beltline through the West End lately, you've probably noticed construction picking up at 600 Hopkins Street. That's GW Parkside, formerly known as Hopkins and White Townhomes, and it's one of the few places you can actually buy something new along this part of the trail.

Ackerman & Co., the same developer behind the Lee + White complex and all its cool food and office offerings, started this project a year ago after demolishing what was there before. The first seven townhomes are really starting to take shape, and they should be ready for buyers this spring.

What makes this project interesting is that there just aren't many for-sale options along the Beltline's southwest stretch. Most of what we've seen has been rental apartments, so having actual townhomes that people can own feels like a big deal for the neighborhood's long-term stability.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes development in West End near Beltline

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. Expect soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis at this pop-up-turned-permanent spot. Both chefs have serious credentials (she worked for Gordon Ramsay in NYC), so this should be good.
stories.body:
You know that stretch of Elizabeth Street in Inman Park where it feels like new restaurants are always popping up? Well, the latest addition is Mushi Ni, and it's from a couple of chefs who definitely know what they're doing. Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez have been doing the pop-up thing for a while, but now they've got their own space just off the Freedom Park Trail.

The menu hits all the right notes for what I want from Asian-American comfort food. The soft shell crab bao sounds incredible, and at $25 for a Peking chicken platter that feeds two people, the pricing seems pretty reasonable for Inman Park. Plus, cocktails like a tamarind whiskey sour and Thai tea martinis show they're thinking beyond just the food.

Jimenez has legit kitchen experience (Gordon Ramsay in New York, then Staplehouse and Gunshow here in Atlanta), so this isn't just another trendy concept without the skills to back it up. The timing feels right too, with Inman Park continuing to evolve as a dining destination beyond just the handful of longtime favorites.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter from Mushi Ni restaurant in Inman Park

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style Brisket to Ansley Mall
stories.summary: The pitmaster behind some of Austin's best BBQ just opened at Ansley Mall behind The Cook's Warehouse, complete with a spectacular upstairs smokehouse overlooking the new Beltline bridge. Six 22-foot smokers run 24/7 to turn out what might be Atlanta's best brisket. The setup alone is worth checking out.
stories.body:
John Lewis has built something pretty special at Ansley Mall, and I'm not just talking about the food (though the brisket is legitimately excellent). The smokehouse upstairs is unlike anything I've seen in Atlanta, with six massive smokers filling a loft space with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the Beltline. It looks more like a fancy engine room than a typical BBQ joint.

Lewis helped weld those smokers out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and they run around the clock in eight-hour shifts. That kind of operation produces the consistency you need for really good brisket, and after trying it, I can say this is some of the best you'll find in the city. The Austin influence is clear, but it works perfectly here.

The location makes sense too. You've got The Cook's Warehouse right there for people who are already thinking about food, plus easy access from the Beltline. It's exactly the kind of serious, craft-focused operation that Ansley Mall needed to establish itself as a real food destination.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter from Lewis Barbecue at Ansley Mall showing brisket and sides

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's Eastside Food Hall Opens Mid-April
stories.summary: The founder of NYC's Gansevoort Market is bringing his third Atlanta food concept to the new Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club. Vela Park Food Works will feature six chef-driven concepts with craft cocktails and outdoor dining, opening in mid-April. This could be exactly what the eastside needs for dining options.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid knows how to do food halls right. The guy created NYC's Gansevoort Market, and he's already proven himself in Atlanta with his other projects. Now he's opening Vela Park Food Works as part of the new rental complex near East Lake Golf Club, and it sounds like it could be a game-changer for eastside dining.

Six different chef-driven concepts under one roof, plus craft cocktails and roomy outdoor dining, is exactly the kind of thing this part of town has been missing. The eastside has great neighborhoods but not nearly enough quality food options that aren't in Little Five Points or Decatur proper.

The timing couldn't be better either. With the Vela Park apartments filling up and more development happening around East Lake, having a serious food destination in the middle of it all should help anchor the whole area. If Montwaid can bring even half of what made Gansevoort Market special, this is going to be worth the trip.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing multiple food concepts and seating areas

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Project Nears Finish
stories.summary: The West Howard Avenue makeover from Downtown Decatur to East Lake MARTA is almost done after nearly a decade of planning. The project adds a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming features along what used to be a high-speed drag strip. It's exactly the kind of infrastructure that makes neighborhoods more walkable.
stories.body:
Anyone who's driven West Howard Avenue in the past knows it was basically a drag strip between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA. Cars flying by at 45+ mph, sketchy sidewalks, and zero consideration for anyone not in a vehicle. Well, that's finally changing thanks to a Complete Streets project that's been in the works since 2017.

The new setup includes a two-way cycle track with actual concrete barriers (not just paint), proper sidewalks, and traffic calming features that should slow cars down to something resembling the speed limit. Work started last August and they're basically done now, covering about a mile from Commerce Drive near Decatur High to Paden Circle by East Lake MARTA.

This is huge for connectivity between Decatur and the eastside neighborhoods. Having a safe, protected bike route all the way to MARTA opens up so many more options for getting around without a car. Plus, anything that makes West Howard less of a highway through a neighborhood is a win for everyone who actually lives there.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lane on West Howard Avenue in Decatur with concrete barriers and fresh pavement

stories.headline: City Council Moves Forward on ICE Restrictions
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced resolutions barring ICE detention facilities in Atlanta and requiring police documentation when ICE agents are present during operations. Meanwhile, dozens of residents criticized Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters' union contract that council already ratified. Full council votes April 20.
stories.body:
Tuesday's Public Safety Committee meeting covered a lot of ground, but two big issues dominated the conversation. First, council members unanimously moved forward with resolutions that would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits and require Atlanta police to document whenever ICE agents are involved in their operations. Both measures head to the full council for a vote on April 20.

But the real fireworks came from dozens of residents who showed up to blast Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever firefighters' union contract. Council ratified the deal back in April 2025, but Dickens has just let it sit on his desk ever since. The firefighters and their supporters made it clear they're not going to let this slide quietly.

The ICE resolutions feel particularly timely given everything happening at the airport right now with federal agents being deployed there. Having clear policies about when and how ICE can operate in the city seems like exactly the kind of thing council should be addressing, especially with the current administration's immigration enforcement priorities.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting with residents in attendance

stories.headline: 7-Year-Old Dies in Accidental Shooting in DeKalb
stories.summary: A 7-year-old boy died after accidentally shooting himself at a home on Loveless Drive in the Ellenwood area. Family members told reporters the child was playing with a firearm when it discharged. It's another tragic reminder of the importance of gun safety, especially around children.
stories.body:
This is the kind of story that just breaks your heart. DeKalb County police responded to a home on Loveless Drive in Ellenwood early Wednesday morning and found a 7-year-old boy who had accidentally shot himself while playing with a gun. He didn't survive.

Family members who gathered at the scene told reporters the boy was playing with the firearm when it went off. Police are treating it as an 'non-intended discharge of a weapon,' which is the technical term, but doesn't make it any less devastating for everyone involved.

This tragedy highlights something we talk about too often in Atlanta. Kids finding unsecured firearms in homes and the terrible consequences that follow. Every gun owner has a responsibility to make sure their weapons are stored safely, especially when children are around. This family will never be the same, and it didn't have to happen.
stories.imageAlt: Police vehicles at scene of accidental shooting investigation in DeKalb County

stories.headline: 8-Foot Python Spotted on Atlanta Beltline
stories.summary: Someone captured video of a man handling a massive python found along the Beltline, but wildlife officials are warning people not to touch unknown animals. The snake was 8-10 feet long and definitely not native to Georgia. Nobody knows if the person who grabbed it was the owner or just a brave (or foolish) passerby.
stories.body:
Just when you think you've seen everything on the Beltline, someone spots an 8-foot python just hanging out in the bushes. Joseph Ragland was walking the trail when he came across what he immediately knew wasn't your typical Georgia snake, and he started filming when some random guy decided to just grab it barehanded.

'I'm walking up behind it trying to see if I can see the head and then somebody's uncle comes out of nowhere and starts yanking it out of the bush,' Ragland explained. Whether the guy was the snake's owner or just someone who thought he knew what he was doing, nobody seems to know.

Jason Clark from Southeastern Reptile Rescue says the video is concerning because handling unknown snakes can be dangerous for both the person and the animal. Pythons aren't venomous, but they're powerful constrictors, and this one was big enough to cause serious damage. If you see wildlife you don't recognize on the Beltline (or anywhere else), just call the professionals.
